fact: June 4 is exactly 155 days into the year, which means there are exactly 210 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for fireflies to start their magical light shows in backyards and forests.
Cherry trees in many parts of North America are dropping their fruit right around now, creating sticky sidewalks that attract swarms of bees and wasps.
The sun rises before 5:30 AM in most northern cities on June 4, meaning some kids wake up thinking it's already afternoon!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, June 4 marks the heart of winter, with temperatures dropping and leaves turning brown and falling.
This date falls in the middle of Gemini season, when the zodiac sign is associated with curiosity and communication.
Mosquitoes reach peak annoyance levels around this time in many warm climates because they love the combination of heat and recent spring rains.
June 4 sits just 17 days before the summer solstice, meaning daylight is stretching longer almost every single day in the north.
Many flowers like roses and peonies are bursting into full bloom right now, making the air smell incredibly sweet in gardens worldwide.
In northern regions, the sun doesn't set until after 8 PM on June 4, giving kids extra hours to play outside after dinner.
Baby birds are leaving their nests for the first time around this date, which means you might spot clumsy young robins hopping around on the ground.
The moon phase on June 4 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, the night sky becomes perfect for spotting shooting stars.
